title: Grammar & Literary Devices
description: Parts of speech, sentence structure, figurative language, and literary techniques

cards.back: Comparison using 'like' or 'as' (e.g., 'brave as a lion')
Simile
Direct comparison without 'like' or 'as' (e.g., 'time is money')
Metaphor
Giving human qualities to non-human things (e.g., 'the wind whispered')

cards.front: Giving human qualities to non-human things
cards.back: Personification
Extreme exaggeration for emphasis (e.g., 'I've told you a million times')
Hyperbole
A word that imitates a sound (e.g., 'buzz,' 'crash,' 'sizzle')

cards.front: A word that imitates a sound
cards.back: Onomatopoeia
Repetition of initial consonant sounds (e.g., 'Peter Piper picked')
Alliteration
Repetition of vowel sounds within words (e.g., 'the rain in Spain')

cards.front: Repetition of vowel sounds within words
cards.back: Assonance
When the opposite of what is expected happens or is said
Verbal (saying the opposite), situational (unexpected outcome), dramatic (audience knows more than characters)
Hints or clues about what will happen later in a story

cards.front: Hints about what will happen later
cards.back: Foreshadowing
A scene that interrupts the present to show events from the past
A story where characters and events represent deeper moral or political meanings (e.g., Animal Farm)
Using an object, person, or event to represent something beyond its literal meaning
Descriptive language that appeals to the five senses
The author's attitude toward the subject or audience
The feeling or atmosphere a text creates for the reader
Tone = author's attitude; Mood = reader's feeling
The central message or underlying meaning of a literary work
The main character of a story
The character or force that opposes the protagonist
Protagonist = main character; Antagonist = opposing force
Narrator is a character in the story, uses 'I' and 'we'

cards.front: Point of view — third person limited
cards.back: Narrator is outside the story but knows one character's thoughts

cards.front: Point of view — third person omniscient
cards.back: Narrator is outside the story and knows all characters' thoughts
Two contradictory words placed together (e.g., 'jumbo shrimp,' 'deafening silence')
Oxymoron
A statement that seems contradictory but reveals a truth (e.g., 'less is more')
A brief reference to a well-known person, event, or work (e.g., 'He was a real Romeo')
Using humor, irony, or exaggeration to criticize or mock
A speech in a play where a character speaks thoughts aloud, alone on stage
A long speech by one character, directed at others on stage
Soliloquy = alone on stage; Monologue = speaking to others
A grouped set of lines in a poem (like a paragraph in prose)
Two consecutive lines of poetry that rhyme
A 14-line poem, usually in iambic pentameter, with a specific rhyme scheme
A three-line Japanese poem with 5-7-5 syllable pattern
A line of verse with five metrical feet, each with an unstressed then stressed syllable
A word that names a person, place, thing, or idea
A word that expresses an action or state of being
A word that describes or modifies a noun
A word that modifies a verb, adjective, or other adverb
A word that shows the relationship between a noun and another word (e.g., in, on, under, between)
A word that connects words, phrases, or clauses (e.g., and, but, or)
A word or phrase that expresses strong emotion (e.g., 'Wow!' 'Ouch!')
The person or thing performing the action
The part of the sentence that tells what the subject does or is
Clause has a subject and verb; phrase does not
A clause that can stand alone as a complete sentence
A clause that cannot stand alone; depends on an independent clause
Repetition of a word or phrase at the beginning of successive clauses (e.g., 'I have a dream…')
A mild or indirect word used in place of something harsh or blunt (e.g., 'passed away' for 'died')
